reasoning: The NBER declares recessions based on broad and significant declines in economic activity. In 2023 and 2024, US economic indicators were robust: real GDP grew by 2.9% in 2023 and 2.8% in 2024, with positive growth in all four quarters of 2024 (including 2.3% in Q4). Furthermore, nonfarm payrolls continued to grow consistently, and while the Sahm Rule triggered a technical recession indicator in July 2024 due to an increasing labor supply, economists widely identified it as a false positive. Given these factors, and the fact that we are in November 2025 with no such NBER declaration having occurred (nor any plausible retroactive peak in 2024 due to strong end-of-year data), the probability of an NBER recession declaration covering 2023 or 2024 by the end of 2025 is exceptionally low.
batch_queries: "NBER Business Cycle Dating Committee" announcement recession 2024
US GDP growth rate Q3 Q4 2024 BEA release
economist consensus Wall Street "US recession in 2024"
Sahm rule trigger unemployment rate US late 2024
US economy "soft landing" avoid recession 2024 review
